source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 10.3.2-10.3.3 cpio read and copy-in (pp. 189-190)

questions.q: Read (list) the contents of the cpio backup /backups/etc.152.cpio with file attributes.
questions.why: -t reads/lists the archive and -v shows attributes; always read a backup before restoring from it.
-i is copy-in (extract/restore); the archive is fed on stdin (or via -F).

questions.q: By default, what happens to a file on disk that is NEWER than (or same age as) its copy in the backup, during a cpio restore?
options: It is NOT restored (to avoid overwriting newer data with older)
It is renamed with a .bak suffix
questions.why: cpio skips files whose on-disk modification date is newer than or equal to the backup, protecting recent data.

questions.q: What does the -u option do during a cpio restore?
options: Replaces all files even if they already exist (overwriting, including with older versions)
Restores to the user's home directory
questions.why: -u forces every file to be replaced, so it can overwrite newer on-disk files with older ones from the archive.

options: Rebuilds the missing directory tree during extraction
questions.why: -d recreates any missing directory structure needed to place the restored files.

questions.q: What does --no-absolute-filenames allow you to do?
options: Restore an absolute-mode backup in a relative way (the leading / is stripped)
Force absolute paths on a relative backup
questions.why: --no-absolute-filenames removes the leading / so an absolute backup restores relative to the current directory.

questions.q: Restore the absolute backup home.A.cpio using the -F option (copy-in, verbose).
questions.why: -F names the archive to read from, so no '<' redirection is needed; -i restores, -v is verbose.
